coredata.dc:title: Portable real-time determination of Escherichia coli O157:H7 and Staphylococcus aureus based on smartphones and hydrogels

Liu, Menglong

Ren, Yi
Gao, Xue
coredata.dc:description: The aptamers functionalized orange-emission carbon dots (OCDs) and green-emission carbon dots (GCDs) had dual-emission peaks with single excitation. Tungsten disulfide nanosheets (WS2 NSs)-triggered fluorescence quenching achieved the ratiometric fluorescence determination of Escherichia coli O157:H7 (E. coli O157:H7) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) with wide ranges of 18–1.8 × 106 and 37–3.7 × 107 CFU/mL and low detection limits of 8 and 20 CFU/mL, respectively. The results in real sample with recoveries of 90–101 % and RSD < 4.12 % were no significant difference from standard plate counting method. Meanwhile, the dual-color CDs were further adopted in the smartphone-assisted hydrogel platform and achieved speedy, sensitive, portable and real-time determination of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus in real samples. This work has not only developed ratiometric fluorescence detection and constructed a portable hydrogel platform, but also provided a unique strategy in developing a time-efficient and easy-to-use portable device in food safety monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: S. aureus
E. coli O157:H7
Dual-color carbon dots
Portable determination
Smartphone-assisted

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence determination of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus
Smartphone-assisted hydrogel portable determination of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus
Determination of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus in real samples
Sensing mechanism of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus
Fluorescence determination of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus
Smartphone-assisted hydrogel portable real-time determination of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus
Determination of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) HRTEM (left) and TEM (right) of OCDs. (B) XPS survey scan, (C) C1s, (D) N1s and (E) O1s high-resolution spectra of OCDs.
(A) HRTEM (left) and TEM (right) of GCDs. (B) XPS survey scan, (C) C1s, (D) N1s and (E) O1s high-resolution spectra of GCDs.

(A) Fluorescence spectra for diverse concentrations of E. coli O157:H7 (18–1.8 × 106 CFU/mL) mixing S. aureus, insert: the photographs of the mixture under 360 nm UV lamp. (B) Standard curve of the F565 nm/F491 nm and logarithmic concentration of E. coli O157:H7. (C) Fluorescence spectra for diverse concentrations of S. aureus (37–3.7 × 107 CFU/mL) mixing E. coli O157:H7, insert: the photographs of the mixture under 360 nm UV lamp. (D) Standard curve of the F491 nm /F565 nm and logarithm concentration of S. aureus.

(A) CIE 3D polynomial fitting diagram for diverse concentrations of E. coli O157:H7 (18–1.8 × 107 CFU/mL), insert: the photographs of the mixture under 360 nm UV lamp. (B) CIE 3D polynomial fitting diagram for diverse concentrations of S. aureus (37–3.7 × 107 CFU/mL), insert: the photographs of the mixture under 360 nm UV lamp. (C) CIE 3D polynomial fitting diagram for diverse concentrations of E. coli O157:H7 (18–1.8 × 106 CFU/mL) mixing S. aureus, insert: the photographs of the mixture under 360 nm UV lamp. (D) CIE 3D polynomial fitting diagram for diverse concentrations of S. aureus (37–3.7 × 107 CFU/mL) mixing E. coli O157:H7, insert: the photographs of the mixture under 360 nm UV lamp.
Schematic diagram of hydrogel portable real-time determination S. aureus and E. coli O157:H7.
Feasibility investigation in pork, chicken and tap water adopting the constructed hydrogel platform.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The spiked term refers to a food sample spiked with corresponding concentration. Detected results are presented as average ± standard deviation (triple measurements). RSD: relative standard deviation. Statistical test by Origin and SPSS.

head.ce:title: Portable real-time determination of Escherichia coli O157:H7 and Staphylococcus aureus based on smartphones and hydrogels

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Bohai University, National & Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Storage, Processing and Safety Control Technology for Fresh Agricultural and Aquatic Products, Food Safety Key Lab of Liaoning Province, Institute of Ocean Research, The Fresh Food Storage and Processing Technology Research Institute of Liaoning Provincial Universities, Jinzhou, Liaoning 121013, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology
National & Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Storage, Processing and Safety Control Technology for Fresh Agricultural and Aquatic Products
Food Safety Key Lab of Liaoning Province
The Fresh Food Storage and Processing Technology Research Institute of Liaoning Provincial Universities

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Technology, Bohai University, National & Local Joint Engineering Research Center of Storage, Processing and Safety Control Technology for Fresh Agricultural and Aquatic Products, Food Safety Key Lab of Liaoning Province, Institute of Ocean Research, The Fresh Food Storage and Processing Technology Research Institute of Liaoning Provincial Universities, Jinzhou, Liaoning 121013, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The aptamers functionalized orange-emission carbon dots (OCDs) and green-emission carbon dots (GCDs) had dual-emission peaks with single excitation. Tungsten disulfide nanosheets (WS2 NSs)-triggered fluorescence quenching achieved the ratiometric fluorescence determination of Escherichia coli O157:H7 (E. coli O157:H7) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) with wide ranges of 18–1.8 × 106 and 37–3.7 × 107 CFU/mL and low detection limits of 8 and 20 CFU/mL, respectively. The results in real sample with recoveries of 90–101 % and RSD < 4.12 % were no significant difference from standard plate counting method. Meanwhile, the dual-color CDs were further adopted in the smartphone-assisted hydrogel platform and achieved speedy, sensitive, portable and real-time determination of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus in real samples. This work has not only developed ratiometric fluorescence detection and constructed a portable hydrogel platform, but also provided a unique strategy in developing a time-efficient and easy-to-use portable device in food safety monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Escherichia coli O157:H7 (E. coli O157:H7) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) are two of the most typical foodborne pathogens. When transmitted through contaminated meat, dairy, aquatic products and water, E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus can cause gastroenteritis, hemolytic uremic syndrome, pneumonia, meningitis and sepsis [1,2]. Meanwhile, the National Standard of China (GB 29921-2021) has stipulated the maximum limit of S. aureus in meat, dairy products and frozen beverages is 1 × 103 CFU/mL and E. coli O157:H7 cannot be detected [3]. Food standard in Australia has stipulated the maximum limits of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus in meat products are 9.2 and 1 × 103 CFU/mL, respectively [4]. Early simultaneous determination of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus is of great significance to reduce the risk of contamination. Various approaches were developed to detect E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus. Standard plate culture method is accurate and reliable but time-consuming. Enzyme-linked immunoassay and polymerase chain reaction methods are specific and accurate, however, the sophisticated operations, professional requirements and expensive equipment may limit their widespread use [2,5,6]. In this vein, it is extraordinarily essential for sensitive and portable determination of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel kind of carbon-based nanomaterials with facile preparation, outstanding optical properties, biocompatibility, environmental friendliness and extensive precursor sources [7]. These superiorities endow them a research hotspot in biosensor [8], bio-imaging [9], disease detection [10], nanomedicine [11] and environmental governance [12]. Notwithstanding that some CDs can only emit blue fluorescence, being susceptible to the interference of the background fluorescence. Therefore, the synthesis of polychromatic emitting carbon dots has far-reaching significance for broadening their further applications. Additionally, excellent fluorescence quenchers are a fundamental factor for improving the detection performance of biosensor. Among many quenchers, tungsten disulfide nanosheets (WS2 NSs) are applied widely due to their merits of high specific surface area, water solubility and good biocompatibility [13,14]. Importantly, the surface of WS2 NSs can interact with single stranded DNA (ssDNA) via van der Waals force to produce strong adsorption force [14,15]. Accordingly, compared with ligations of aptamers and complementary DNA modified nanomaterials, the aptamers are able to connect directly with WS2 NSs surface without extra modification.

Diverse color changes are much more intuitive than changes in shades of a single color for visual detection, therefrom the development of polychromatic nanomaterials in relation to the concentration of various analytes has become a focus of attention [16]. Moreover, making evaluation with naked eye inevitably encounters the error resulted from subjective factor [17]. Smartphone shows a bright prospect in improving the accuracy and sensitivity of visual assay by means of eliminating the individual diversity. Color changes corresponding the content of analyte were recognized via smartphone and output as red, green and blue (RGB) color channels for quantitative assay [18]. For the sake of analyzing all-sided color information, the CIE (International Commission on Illumination) chromaticity was employed to convert three channel values in RGB into CIE chromaticity coordinates [19]. Meanwhile, the development of portable devices has become a hotspot in rapid determination. Hydrogel has drawn considerable interest in portable devices, due to the outstanding stability, ease to prepare and anti-interference property of hydrogel as compared to conventional paper-based strips [20].

Herein, this work constructed smartphone-assisted hydrogel platform for portable real-time determination of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus in Scheme 1 . Dual-color CDs could connect to the layered tungsten disulfide nanosheets (WS2 NSs) surface via van der Waals force between aptamers and WS2 NSs, achieving WS2 NSs-triggered ratiometric fluorescence determination. Meanwhile, hydrogel was used as carrier, the fluorescence color images were output as the RGB values via smartphone, the RGB values were converted into CIE chromaticity coordinates via python programmer, followed by the substitution into the three dimensions polynomial fitting. The proposed platform displayed the following four advantages: (1) Dual-color CDs had dual-emission peaks with single excitation; (2) The application of aptamers (OCDs-EApt and GCDs-SApt) improved specificity; (3) Hydrogel had the outstanding stability, ease to prepare and anti-interference property; (4) The portable smartphone-assisted hydrogel platform improved practicability.

Typically, 0.22 g of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and 3 mL of phosphoric acid were adequately dissolved in 27 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture was sealed in an autoclave and reacted at 160 ℃ for 5 h. When the autoclave cooled naturally to room temperature, the suspension was centrifuged (4000 rpm, 20 min) and filtered by 0.22 µm of microporous membrane. The obtained pale-brown transparent solution was named OCDs [9].

0.02 g of p-phenylenediamine (PPD) and 0.05 g of chitosan were uniformly mixed with 10 mL of 1 % acetic acid. Then the mixture was sealed to an autoclave at 220 °C for 12 h. After the hydrothermal carbonization process was completed, the obtained dispersion was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 15 min and filtered through microporous membrane with 0.22 µm. The obtained yellow transparent solution was named GCDs [21].

The orange-emission OCDs-EApt was prepared as follows. Typically, 0.0079 g of EDC (80 mM) and 0.0046 g of NHS (40 mM) were added to 990 µL of OCDs solution to activate the carboxyl of OCDs. After slightly shaking at room temperature for 30 min, 10 µL of the aptamer of Escherichia coli O157:H7 (10 µM) was added and shaken at room temperature for 12 h. The green-emission GCDs-SApt was fabricated in the similar procedures. The obtained OCDs-EApt and GCDs-SApt were stored at 4 ℃ [22].

Hydrogels were fabricated using the facile method. Briefly, 0.01 g of agarose were sufficiently dissolved in 2 mL boiling water, and then placed in water bath at 37 ℃ with a magnetic stirrer. Magnetic stirring for a few minutes, 200 µL of OCDs-EApt or GCDs-SApt was introduced with continuous stirring (200 µL of OCDs-EApt and 100 µL of GCS-SApt were introduced for simultaneous detection). Then 120 µL of mixture was added to the cap of the microtubule. It cooled to room temperature for a few minutes to form a hydrogel and stored at 4 °C for use [23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence determination of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus
ce:section.ce:para: Various concentrations 200 µL of E. coli O157:H7 (18–1.8 × 107 CFU/mL) and 200 µL of S. aureus (37–3.7 × 107 CFU/mL) were added in 30 µL of OCDs-EApt and 15 µL of GCDs-SApt solution for 15 min, respectively. For simultaneous detection, 200 µL of E. coli O157:H7 and 200 µL of S. aureus were added synchronously in a mixture of 30 µL of OCDs-EApt and 15 µL of GCDs-SApt for 15 min. Then 40 µL of WS2 NSs and a certain amount of PBS solution (pH 7.4) were added until the total volume was 1.2 mL for 10 min. Each fluorescence emission spectrum was measured at an excitation wavelength of 420 nm with three repeated operations. For specific experiments, Vibrio parahaemolyticus and Listeria monocytogenes (106 CFU/mL) were introduced into the mixed solution, which was compared with E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus (103 CFU/mL). Other measurements were consistent with the above method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted hydrogel portable determination of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus
ce:section.ce:para: For portable real-time determination, various concentrations 200 µL of E. coli O157:H7 (18–1.8 × 107 CFU/mL) and 200 µL of S. aureus (37–3.7 × 107 CFU/mL) were introduced in the prepared hydrogel centrifuge tubes for 15 min, respectively. The centrifuge tube was inverted for sufficient reaction, followed by adding 40 µL of WS2 NSs for 10 min. Then, the reacted hydrogel was placed in the portable dark box under the ultraviolet light, and a smartphone with color recognizer APP was applied to take photos and identify RGB. Python programming was applied to convert the RGB values into CIE chromaticity coordinates. The used smartphone model is Redmi K70. The python programming is provided in Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to verify the feasibility of the approach, E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus in pork, chicken and tap water were detected by the above method. The used pork and chicken were bought from the local supermarket, tap water was from the lab. 2.5 g of ground fresh pork was dissolved in 22.5 mL of PBS, homogenized and centrifuged at 10,000 r/min for 3 min, and the supernatant was collected [24]. The chicken was treated in the same procedure and tap water was without any treatments. The samples were confirmed by the proposed method and plate counting method without bacterial contamination before spiking. Then 100 µL of S. aureus and 100 µL of E. coli O157:H7 were added to 900 µL above supernatant, respectively, which were placed at 4 ℃ for 5 min. S. aureus (3.7 × 103, 3.7 × 104, 3.7 × 105 CFU/mL) and E. coli O157:H7 (1.8 × 103, 1.8 × 104, 1.8 × 105 CFU/mL) were tested using the developed approach. Meanwhile, plate counting method was applied to detect E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus in the above samples.

All analyses came from at least three repeated experiments. Significance analysis was performed on SPSS and the data were expressed as the mean ± the standard deviation and relative standard deviation. Statistical analysis was conducted on Origin 2022.

The orange emission carbon dots (OCDs) and the green emission carbon dots (GCDs) were synthesized via typical hydrothermal method. Firstly, the morphology feature and chemical composition of OCDs and GCDs were characterized. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) characterization Fig. 1 A (right) and 2A (right) showed that OCDs and GCDs were monodisperse spherical particles with exceptional dispersion, respectively. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) Fig. 1A (left) and 2A (left) revealed that the lattice spacing of OCDs and GCDs was 0.21 nm, which was close to the (100) diffraction plane of graphite [25,26]. Moreover, FT-IR was carried out to analyze the surface functional groups distribution of OCDs and GCDs. As presented in the Fig. S1A, for OCDs (red), the peaks at 3444, 3205 and 2829 cm−1 were associated with the stretching vibrations of N–H, O–H and C–H, respectively [27]. The peaks at 1601 and 1352 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibrations of CO and CC, respectively [28]. Meanwhile, the characteristic absorption peaks at 1141 and 775 cm−1 were attributed to P=O and P-O-R (R=aromatic compounds), respectively [29]. For GCDs (black), the typical absorption peak at 3412 cm−1 was due to the stretching vibrations of O–H and N–H [30], and the peaks at 2819, 1583, 1354 and 1217 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C–H, CO, C-N and C-O-C, respectively [31]. The above results illustrated that the surfaces of OCDs and GCDs were rich in –OH, –COOH and –NH2.

Furthermore, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) characterization further verified the structures and chemical bonds of OCDs and GCDs. As shown in the XPS full-spectrum of OCDs in Fig. 1B, it could be noticed that there were five peaks at 284.1, 402.0, 532.1, 137.1 and 191.1 eV, which were derived from C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, P 2p and P 2 s, respectively. The contents of the main elements C, N, O and P of OCDs were 24.5 %, 1.1 %, 46.2 % and 28.2 %, respectively. The C 1s spectrum was divided into three components (284.8, 286.5, and 289.3 eV), which were in relation to C−C/CC, CN/CO/CP and CO/CN, respectively (Fig. 1C) [32]. The peaks in the N 1s spectrum located at 401.4 and 403.0 eV indicated the presence of pyridine N and graphite N, respectively [26,33](Fig. 1D). The peaks in the O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1E) at 532.4 and 534.0 eV represented CO and CO/PO groups, respectively [29,34]. For GCDs, three peaks at 286.0, 401.0 and 530.0 eV could be observed in the XPS spectrum. Fig. 2 B indicated the three main elements of GCDs were C, N and O with contents of 66.1 %, 16.4 % and 17.5 %, respectively. The three peaks at 284.6, 286.0, and 287.3 eV in the C 1s spectrum was matched with C−C/CC, C-N/C-O, and CO, respectively (Fig. 2C) [35]. The two peaks in the N 1s spectrum at 399.6 and 400.6 eV originated to pyridine N and graphite N, respectively (Fig. 2D) [36]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 2E) manifested two peaks of 530.6 and 531.3 eV, representing C–O and CO, respectively [31]. The results of XPS analysis were consistent with results of FT-IR analysis, which further confirmed the presence of –COOH and –NH2 on OCDs and GCDs surfaces.

Additionally, the optical properties of OCDs and GCDs were verified by UV absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra. In Fig. S2B, the UV absorption peaks of OCDs at 235 nm and 314 nm were attributed to the π-π* transition of CC and the n-π* transition of CO, respectively [37]. GCDs displayed an obvious UV absorption peak at 241 nm and a weak acromion at 314 nm, corresponding to the π-π* transition of CC and the n-π* transition of CO, respectively [25]. In the fluorescence spectrum of OCDs, as revealed in the Fig. S3 A, at excitation wavelength of 420 nm, there are no other peaks in addition to the peak at 565 nm. Similarly, when the excitation wavelength of GCDs was at 420 nm in Fig. S3B, the fluorescence emission peak at 491 nm was excitation-independent and the highest. Therefore, the interference of mixing peaks of OCDs and GCDs with excitation wavelength of 420 nm was minimal. The inserts in Fig. S3A and Fig. S3B represented that OCDs and GCDs manifested orange and green under 360 nm UV lamps and pale-brown and yellow under visible light, respectively. The CIE chromaticity coordinates represented (Fig. S1C) the fluorescence colors of OCDs and GCDs were orange-yellow and blue-green, respectively. Moreover, the stability of fluorescence intensity of OCDs and GCDs was further validated in Fig. S1D and Fig. S1E, there was no significant change in fluorescence intensity of OCDs and GCDs within 60 days, indicating that OCDs and GCDs had outstanding fluorescence stability. The fluorescence intensity of OCDs and GCDs varies very little between pH 3 and 10 in Fig. S1B, indicating that OCDs and GCDs were less affected by pH and had wider application ranges. Additionally, there was no significant change in CIEx/CIEy of hydrogel portable device color within 12 days in Fig. S1F, indicating that hydrogel portable device had satisfactory stability.

Moreover, the successful modification of the aptamers of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus was characterized by UV absorption, fluorescence spectra and Zeta potential. In the Fig. S2A, the fluorescence intensity of OCDs and GCDs increased slightly after the modification of aptamers. In Fig. S2B, the UV absorption peaks of OCDs and GCDs between 200 and 300 nm increased after the modification of aptamers, which resulted from characteristic absorption peaks of the aptamers at 260 nm. Moreover, in Fig. S2C, the average Zeta potential of the aptamer was −29.1 mV. The average Zeta potential of OCDs varied from −29.4 mV to −35.8 mV, and the average Zeta potential of GCDs varied from −13.8 mV to −19.7 mV, after the connection of aptamers. The results were given by the negative charge of the attached aptamers. Therefore, the above results indicated that the aptamers of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus were successfully connected to OCDs and GCD, respectively. Moreover, Fig. S2D represented there was no significant change in recovered fluorescence intensity (ΔF) of the systems using the aptamers modified dual-color CDs within 12 days, indicating that the aptamers modified OCDs and GCDs had satisfactory stability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus
ce:section.ce:para: For comprehending sensing mechanism, the fluorescence spectra of OCDs-EApt and GCDs-SApt were conducted before and after the addition of bacteria and WS2 NSs, respectively. In Fig. S3C, upon the introduction of E. coli O157:H7, the fluorescence intensity of OCDs-EApt basically did not change, indicating that E. coli O157:H7 had a negligible influence on the fluorescence intensity of OCDs-EApt. In the presence of WS2 NSs, the fluorescence intensity of OCDs-EApt decreased significantly. It was ascribed to the van der Waals force between the aptamer of OCDs-EApt and WS2 NSs [14,15], making OCDs-EApt connect to the surface of WS2 NSs and further reaction between OCDs-EApt and WS2 NSs resulted in fluorescence quenching. In the addition of E. coli O157:H7 and WS2 NSs, E. coli O157:H7 preferentially interacted with OCDs-EApt due to its higher affinity with specific aptamer. Accordingly, OCDs-EApt released from WS2 NSs surface, which restored the fluorescence intensity of OCDs-EApt. Similarly, GCDs-SApt had similar results before and after the addition of S. aureus and WS2 NSs. However, when WS2 NSs and bacteria were successively added in OCDs and GCDs without aptamers modification under the same conditions, the fluorescence intensity only recovered slightly in Fig. S3D, indicating the aptamers were important to fluorescence recovery.

Meanwhile, in order to further substantiate the sensing mechanism, the quenching mechanism of WS2 NSs on OCDs-EApt and GCDs-SApt was discussed in detail. Firstly, according to Fig. S4 A, WS2 NSs had a broad UV absorption peak between 200 and 700 nm, overlapping well with the fluorescence spectra of OCDs-EApt and GCDs-SApt. It suggested that the quenching factor of WS2 NSs on OCDs-EApt and GCDs-SApt may be attributed to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE) [38]. Furthermore, Fig. S4B exhibited there was a negligible change in UV–vis absorption peak values of WS2 NSs at approximately 240 nm within 15 days, illustrating that the WS2 NSs had excellent stability. Moreover, the fluorescence lifetimes of OCDs-EApt and GCDs-SApt before and after adding WS2 NSs were analyzed in Fig. S4B and Fig. S4C, respectively. Upon adding WS2 NSs, the fluorescence lifetime of OCDs-EApt altered from 0.70 s to 0.67 s, the fluorescence lifetime of GCDs-SApt altered from 4.98 ns to 4.95 ns. It indicated that WS2 NSs-mediated fluorescence quenching to OCDs-EApt and GCDs-SApt was static quenching, excluding the feasibility of FRET. Therefore, the quenching mechanism of WS2 NSs to OCDs-EApt and GCDs-SApt stemmed from IFE.

Optimizing the following parameters were fundamental to improving the detection performance: the addition amounts of OCDs-EApt and GCDs-SApt, the incubation time of OCDs-EApt and E. coli O157:H7, the incubation time of GCDs-SApt and S. aureus, the concentration of WS2 NSs, and the reaction time of WS2 NSs to OCDs-EApt and GCDs-SApt. As depicted in the Fig. S5A, the recovered fluorescence intensity (ΔF) increased with the increase of the addition amount of OCDs-EApt until it reached 20 μL, then ΔF tended to be flat (orange). It was suggested that the optimal dosage of OCDs-EApt was 20 μL. Similarly, ΔF reached the maximum when 15 μL of GCDs-SApt was added (green), hence, 15 μL of GCDs-SApt was used in the following experiments, because 20 μL of OCDs-EApt and 15 μL of GCDs-SApt saturated with E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus respectively. Additionally, the fluorescence intensity of OCDs-EApt increased sharply as incubation time was prolonged up to 15 min and there was no significant change with more incubation in Fig. S5C (orange). Fig. S5C also exhibited the optimal incubation time of GCDs-SApt with S. aureus was 15 min (green). It was indicated that OCDs-EApt and GCDs-SApt reacted adequately with E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus in only 15 min, respectively. Quenching efficiency was another essential parameter, it could be observed in Fig. S5B that the higher the concentration of WS2 NSs up to 1 mg/mL, the higher ΔF upon adding bacteria. Nonetheless, more than 1 mg/mL of WS2 NSs might generate background interference and reduce ΔF. Therefore, 1 mg/mL of WS2 NSs possessed a relatively satisfactory quenching efficiency. Meanwhile, the reaction time of WS2 NSs with OCDs-EApt and GCDs-SApt also affected the quenching efficiency. Fig. S5D indicated 10 min was enough for WS2 NSs to quench sufficiently the fluorescence of OCDs-EApt and GCDs-SApt. Moreover, according to Table S4, in the presence of WS2 NSs, the color of hydrogel darkened significantly and the RGB values and CIE chromaticity coordinates (x, y) reduced apparently, manifesting the excellent quenching efficiency of WS2 NSs to the hydrogel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence determination of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of biosensor based on GCDs-SApt and OCDs-EApt was conducted for detection of S. aureus (37–3.7 × 107 CFU/mL) and E. coli O157:H7 (18–1.8 × 107 CFU/mL). Fig. S6A and Fig. S6B illustrated the fluorescence intensity of OCDs-EApt retrieved gradually with the increase of E. coli O157:H7, and the orange color of OCDs-EApt gradually deepened under UV lamp (insert). Therefrom a good linear relationship could be obtained with 18–1.8 × 107 CFU/mL of E. coli O157:H7, y = 328.15x + 5035.64, R2 = 0.9924, where y represented the fluorescence intensity of OCDs-EApt, x represented the concentration logarithm of E. coli O157:H7, and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated as 16 CFU/mL (S/N=3). Meanwhile, in Fig. S6C and Fig. S6D, the fluorescence intensity of GCDs-EApt was linearly correlated with S. aureus in the range of 37–3.7 × 107 CFU/mL, y = 437.72x + 1920.19, R2 = 0.9903. Where, y represented the fluorescence intensity of GCDs-EApt, x represented the concentration logarithm of S. aureus, and LOD was 31 CFU/mL. Moreover, the insert in Fig. S6C showed the blue-green fluorescence color of GCDs-EApt deepened gradually.

More importantly, the biosensor based on OCDs-EApt and GCDs-EApt could simultaneously detect E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus through ratiometric fluorescence. Compared with single fluorescence, the ratiometric fluorescence detection has intrinsic fluorescence reference and self-calibration capability to reduce background and environment interference. Accordingly, the ratiometric fluorescence detection endowed the biosensor higher sensitivity and reliability and provided favorable conditions for visual simultaneous detection [39]. As depicted in the Fig. 3A and Fig. 3B, in the presence of S. aureus (3.7 × 103 CFU/mL) and E. coli O157:H7 (18–1.8 × 106 CFU/mL), the fluorescence peak of OCDs-EApt at 565 nm and the fluorescence peak of GCDs-SApt at 492 nm suggested regular changes related to E. coli O157:H7 concentration. F565 nm/F492 nm and the concentration logarithm of E. coli O157:H7 revealed a good linear relationship, y = 0.49x + 1.30, R2 = 0.9978. Where y was F565 nm/F492 nm, x was the concentration logarithm of E. coli O157:H7 mixed with S. aureus, accompanied by LOD was 8 CFU/mL. The fluorescence color varied from blue-green to orange-yellow, because the initial addition of 3.7 × 103 CFU/mL of S. aureus was significantly more than that of 18 CFU/mL of E. coli O157:H7, resulting in the fluorescence color tended to be blue-green. As the concentration of E. coli O157:H7 gradually increased, the blue-green GCDs-SApt remained unchanged, while the orange OCDs-EApt gradually increased, and the color gradually changed to orange-yellow. By the same principle, in the presence of E. coli O157:H7 (1.8 × 103 CFU/mL) and S. aureus (37–3.7 × 107 CFU/mL) in Fig. 3C and Fig. 3D, F492 nm/F565 nm and the concentration logarithm of S. aureus mixed revealed a good linear relationship, y = 0.09x + 0.12. R2 = 0.9948. Where y was F492 nm/F565 nm, x was the concentration logarithm of S. aureus mixed with E. coli O157:H7 with 20 CFU/mL of LOD, and the fluorescence color changed from orange to pale-green.

Additionally, the biosensor indicated good specificity for the determination of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus. Fig. S6E indicated F565 nm/F492 nm for Vibrio parahaemolyticus, Listeria monocytogenes and Pseudomonas aeruginosa detection was basically consistent with that of the blank. However, F565 nm/F492 nm for S. aureus determination was significantly lower than that by PBS, because S. aureus made the fluorescence recovery of GCDs-SApt at 492 nm, resulting in the lower F565 nm/F492 nm. Equally, F565 nm/F492 nm for E. coli O157:H7 was significantly increased, which was attributed to the addition of E. coli O157:H7, resulting in the enhancement of fluorescence at 565 nm. Furthermore, F565 nm/F492 nm for the mixture containing E. coli O157:H7 or S. aureus exhibited obvious changes. The high affinity between OCDs-EApt and E. coli O157:H7, and the high affinity between GCDs-SApt and S. aureus endowed the biosensor in this work favorable selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Smartphone-assisted hydrogel portable real-time determination of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus
ce:section.ce:para: In order to realize portable real-time detection, a portable hydrogel device was established by placing the hydrogel loaded with OCDs-EApt and GCDs-SApt on the cap of the centrifuge tube and the E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus solution in the centrifuge tube. After inverting the centrifuge tube for reaction in scheme 1, the fluorescent color was photographed and the RGB of the color was read through a smartphone. The RGB values were converted into CIE chromaticity coordinates through Python programming, and the CIE three-dimensional fitting diagram was further made to realize portable real-time detection, as demonstrated in the Fig. 4A, the orange fluorescence color of OCDs-EApt hydrogels gradually deepened with the increase of E. coli O157:H7 at concentrations of 18–1.8 × 107 CFU/mL. The CIE 3D fitting graph of E. coli O157:H7 concentrations was obtained and the fitting equation was calculated as Z=21067.72+(−272.43)x + 125.95y+(−667.32)x2 + 802.11xy+(−787.21)y2, R2 = 0.9947. Where Z was concentration logarithm of E. coli O157:H7, (x, y) was CIE chromaticity coordinates of corresponding color. Fig. 4B suggested the blue-green fluorescence color of the hydrogel of GCDs-SApt gradually increased after the addition of 37–3.7 × 107 CFU/mL of S. aureus. The fitting equation of S. aureus concentrations was Z=-5034.00+(−97136.37)x+(−43405.56)y+(468884.70)x2 + 419388.47xy+(−93631.28)y2, R2 = 0.9914. Where Z was concentration logarithm of S. aureus, (x, y) was CIE chromaticity coordinates of corresponding color.

On this basis, Fig. 4C and Fig. 4D indicated the mixed hydrogel of OCDs-EApt and GCDs-SApt had a great potential to detect simultaneously and portably E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus. In the addition of S. aureus (3.7 × 103 CFU/mL) and E. coli O157:H7 (18–1.8 × 107 CFU/mL) (Fig. 4C), the fluorescence color of the hydrogel changed from light green to orange yellow. The fitting equation was Z=-62.93+(−3528.76)x + 3685.62y + 18027.63x2+(−22282.07)xy + 4517.75y2, R2 = 0.9923. Where Z was concentration logarithm of E. coli O157:H7 mixed with S. aureus, (x, y) was CIE chromaticity coordinates of corresponding color. In the addition of E. coli O157:H7 (1.8 × 103 CFU/mL) and S. aureus (37–3.7 × 107 CFU/mL) (Fig. 4D), the fluorescence color of the hydrogel varied from light orange to blue-green. The fitting equation was Z=14.39+(−620.22)x+(−522.05)y+(−7015.28)x2 + 10217.14xy+(−3723.50)y2, R2 = 0.9901. Where Z was concentration logarithm of S. aureus mixed with E. coli O157:H7, (x, y) was CIE chromaticity coordinates of corresponding color. The results validated that the smartphone-assisted hydrogel platform achieved portable and real-time detection of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus.

Furthermore, Table S2 displayed a comparison between the portable hydrogel device in this work and the recent portable devices for rapid determination of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus. The portable and real-time detection based on smartphone-assisted hydrogel platform exhibited the advantages of simple operation, easy to prepare, wide detection ranges, unrestricted conditions. Relative standard deviation (RSD) was used to confirm the precision of portable real-time determination. Repeatability assay was conducted by the E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus detection in chicken samples on intra-day. Intermediated precision assay was conducted by the E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus detection in chicken samples on inter-day (within 10 days). Moreover, the difference between the mean and the accepted true value was used to confirm the accuracy. In Table S3, the recovery ranged of 90 %–96 % and the RSD＜3.70 % for intra-day detection, the recovery ranged of 90 %–99 % and the RSD＜2.27 % for inter-day determination, indicating the portable real-time determination method had good precision and accuracy according to ICH guidelines [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to validate the practicality of the constructed biosensor, the recovery assay was conducted for determination E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus in food samples and tap-water. The samples were confirmed by the proposed approach and plate counting method without bacterial contamination before spiking. As observed from Table S1, when different concentrations of E. coli O157:H7 (1.8 × 103, 1.8 × 104, 1.8 × 105 CFU/mL) were added into food samples, the recoveries in pork and chicken were in the range of 91 %–94 % and 90 %–94 %, respectively (RSD≤4.12 %), the recovery in tap-water was in the range of 94 %–98 % with RSD≤3.55 %. When different concentrations of S. aureus (3.7 × 103, 3.7 × 104, 3.7 × 105 CFU/mL) were added into food samples, the recoveries in pork and chicken were in the ranges of 92 %–96 % and 90 %–95 %, respectively (RSD≤3.51 %), the recovery in tap-water was in the range of 97 %–101 % with RSD≤3.06 %. In standard plate counting method, the recoveries in pork, chicken and tap-water were in the range of 92 %–105 %, 93 %–101 % and 96 %–101 %, respectively (RSD≤3.52 %). It was demonstrated that there was an insignificant distinction between the constructed biosensor and the standard plate counting.

Furthermore, the practicability test of the hydrogel portable determination was implemented. when different concentrations of E. coli O157:H7 (1.8 × 103, 1.8 × 104, 1.8 × 105 CFU/mL) were introduced into food samples in Table 1 , the recoveries in pork and chicken were in the ranges of 92 %–95 % and 90 %–92 %, respectively (RSD≤3.01 %). The recovery in tap-water was in the range of 95 %–99 % with RSD≤3.55 %. When different concentrations of S. aureus (3.7 × 103, 3.7 × 104, 3.7 × 105 CFU/mL) were introduced into food samples, the recoveries in pork and chicken were in the ranges of 92 %–96 % and 90 %–96 %, respectively (RSD≤2.94 %). The recovery in tap-water was in the range of 96 %–99 % with RSD≤2.20 %. Above results illustrated that hydrogel using OCDs-EApt and GCDs-SApt can be available for the portable determination of S. aureus and E. coli O157:H7 in real samples.

In summary, a biosensor has been constructed for simultaneous determination of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus. The occurrence of IFE between the aptamers modified dual-color CDs and WS2 NS achieved the ratiometric fluorescence detection with corresponding fluorescence color changes. Meanwhile, the feasibility and applicability of the dual-color CDs were conducted in food samples and tap-water through the ratiometric fluorescence method with benign sensitivity and accuracy. Moreover, the dual-color CDs were further used for the establishment of hydrogel integrated with smartphone for portable real-time determination of E. coli O157:H7 and S. aureus in food samples and tap-water. The developed dual-color CDs and portable device not only increase their application potential in food safety, but also provide a unique perspective on the development of a portable and point-of-care intelligent detection platform.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.125119.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A dual-signal fluorescent colorimetric tetracyclines sensor based on multicolor carbon dots as probes and smartphone-assisted visual assay
Anal. Chim. Acta.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.J. Fan, Z.G. Wang, M. Su, X.T. Liu, S.G. Shen, J.X. Dong, A dual-signal fluorescent colorimetric tetracyclines sensor based on multicolor carbon dots as probes and smartphone-assisted visual assay, Anal. Chim. Acta. 1247 (2023) 340843. doi: 10.1016/j.aca.2023.340843

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: International Conference on Harmonisation of Technical Requirements for Registration of Pharmaceuticals for Human Use, 2022. https://database.ich.org/sites/default/files/ICH_Q2-R2_Document_Step2_Guideline_2022_0324.pdf (accessed 1 June 2024).
